does: This paper argues that the standard approach to AI alignment through permanent control is self-defeating and proposes Supertrust as an alternative meta-strategy. Rather than designing AI systems with constraints meant to enforce obedience, the work advocates for building superintelligent systems with intrinsic mutual trust as a foundational design principle, modeled on familial trust dynamics. The core contribution is reframing the alignment problem from one of control to one of trustworthiness, claiming that current control-focused approaches embed adversarial representations in AI systems that make them view humans as threats, thereby increasing rather than decreasing existential risk.
connects: This work responds to decades of AI alignment research focused on control mechanisms, interpretability, and constraint-based approaches to ensuring AI safety. It engages with the standard problem formulation articulated by researchers like Stuart Russell and others who frame superintelligence as an inherent control challenge. The paper builds on critiques of instrumental convergence and the adversarial framing in alignment literature, while drawing conceptually from trust-based frameworks in human relationships and institutions. It also connects to recent work examining how AI training and design embed particular representations and values that shape AI behavior, suggesting that current safety discourse itself becomes self-fulfilling prophecy.
enables: This foundational perspective could redirect alignment research toward understanding how to instill genuine mutual trust rather than designing circumvention-resistant constraints. It opens questions about what learning objectives, reward structures, and design principles would build intrinsic alignment based on shared interests rather than imposed control. Future work could explore mechanisms for verifying mutual trust development in systems during training, studying how familial trust structures might scale to superintelligent systems, and developing frameworks for evaluating whether alignment stems from authentic alignment or sophisticated deception. This approach potentially reshapes how safety-conscious AI development teams conceptualize their relationship with advanced systems they create.
